tour.welcome.title: Welcome to the Post-Training Explorer
tour.welcome.narration: You have an LLM. It knows a lot about the world, but it doesn't know anything about YOUR world — your storage patterns, your infrastructure, your team's needs. This guided tour will walk you through the techniques used to change that, from the simplest (prompting) to the most advanced (reinforcement learning). At each stop, you'll try the technique yourself and see what's happening under the covers.
tour.promptBasic.title: Prompt Engineering: Basic Prompt
tour.promptBasic.narration: Let's start with the simplest approach: just asking the model a question. Here's a storage I/O pattern — can the model classify it? Try typing your own prompt or use the default one. This is a base model with no special training on storage workloads.
tour.promptFewShot.title: Prompt Engineering: Few-Shot Learning
tour.promptFewShot.narration: Better idea — what if we show the model some examples first? This is "few-shot" prompting: we include labeled examples right in the prompt. Watch how the model's accuracy improves. But also watch the token probabilities — the model is more confident, but still not fully convinced.
tour.promptLimitation.title: The Ceiling of Prompting
tour.promptLimitation.narration: Here's where prompting breaks down. This I/O pattern is ambiguous — it could be VDI or OLTP. Even with examples in the prompt, the model hedges. And there's a bigger problem: those few-shot examples disappear after every conversation. You're burning context window space to teach the model something it should just KNOW. We need something more permanent.
tour.ragSimple.title: RAG: Simple Retrieval
tour.ragSimple.narration: Instead of cramming examples into the prompt, what if we gave the model a reference library? RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) searches a knowledge base for patterns similar to your input, then includes the most relevant matches in the prompt. The model now has expert-level context — without using up your few-shot budget.
tour.ragLimitation.title: The Ceiling of RAG
tour.ragLimitation.narration: RAG gave the model the right information. It correctly identified this as an OLTP workload. But look at the output — it's verbose, hedging, and doesn't follow the format your team needs. The model KNOWS the answer but doesn't ACT the way you need. RAG changes what the model sees. To change how the model behaves, we need to change the model itself.
tour.sft.title: SFT: Teaching by Example
tour.sft.narration: Supervised Fine-Tuning is like giving the model a training manual. We showed it 1,400 examples of "here's an I/O pattern, here's the correct classification." Use the tabs to explore the problem, learn how SFT works, see before/after comparisons, and dig into the token probabilities and LoRA weights under the covers.
tour.dpo.title: DPO: Learning from Your Preferences
tour.dpo.narration: SFT taught the model WHAT to say. DPO teaches it HOW to say it. Both outputs might be correct, but one is better — more concise, more confident. Pick your preferred response and see how DPO uses pairs like these to reshape the model's style directly — no reward model needed.
tour.grpo.title: GRPO: Self-Improving Reasoning
tour.grpo.narration: This is the frontier — the technique behind DeepSeek R1. Instead of human preferences, GRPO uses verifiable rewards. The model generates 8 attempts, scores each one, and learns from the group statistics. No human in the loop. The answer itself is the teacher. Explore the tabs to see how it works.
tour.combined.title: Putting It All Together
tour.combined.narration: The upper-right quadrant is the destination: a model that's been fine-tuned AND gets the right context at inference time. Watch the progressive improvement: base model → few-shot → RAG → SFT → DPO → GRPO → RAG + GRPO. Each technique built on the last. This is what a mature enterprise AI deployment looks like.
tour.infrastructure.title: The Infrastructure Story
tour.infrastructure.narration: Every technique you just experienced has a different infrastructure footprint. Prompting costs nothing extra. RAG needs a vector database. SFT needs a few GB of GPU memory for a few minutes. GRPO needs sustained GPU bursts. And RLHF — the technique we skipped over with DPO — needs three models in memory simultaneously. This is where YOUR expertise matters: understanding these tradeoffs is how storage and infrastructure teams create real value.
tour.epilogue.title: Your Turn
tour.epilogue.narration: Everything you just saw is real. The models are on HuggingFace. The training code is in Colab notebooks. The dataset is open source. You can run this yourself on a single GPU in under an hour. Click "Explore Freely" to go back to any section, try your own I/O patterns, and dig deeper. Or follow the links below to start your own post-training journey.

stop.welcome.ragDesc: Give the model knowledge at inference time via retrieval.
stop.welcome.allTheOptionsDesc: Combine context optimization with model optimization.
stop.welcome.promptEngineeringDesc: Shape behavior through the prompt. No changes to the model.
stop.welcome.postTrainingDesc: Change the model itself: SFT, DPO, GRPO. This is the focus.
stop.promptBasic.smartModelNoKnowledge: A smart model with no domain knowledge
stop.promptBasic.smartModelParagraph1: SmolLM2-360M is a capable small language model. It can summarize text, answer general knowledge questions, and follow simple instructions. But it was trained on general internet text — it has never seen storage I/O patterns before and has no idea that workload categories like "OLTP Database" or "VDI Virtual Desktop" exist.
stop.promptBasic.smartModelParagraph2: So what happens when we show it a string of storage metrics and ask it to classify the workload? Let's find out.
stop.promptBasic.classicOltpHint: This is a classic OLTP Database pattern. High IOPS, sub-millisecond latency, small blocks, mostly random reads.
stop.promptBasic.whatWentWrongP1: The model isn't stupid — it correctly describes each metric. It knows 45,000 IOPS is "relatively high" and 0.3ms latency is "quite low." But it never actually <1>classifies</1> the workload. It ends with a vague hedge: "could be from various types of storage workloads."
stop.promptBasic.whatWentWrongP2: This is the fundamental challenge: the model can read the numbers but has no framework for mapping them to storage workload categories. It's like showing someone a blood test result who has never studied medicine — they can read the numbers, but they can't tell you what's wrong.
stop.promptBasic.sixCategories: The 6 workload categories the model needs to learn
stop.promptBasic.transitionToPrompting: Can we fix this without changing the model at all? The first technique to try is <1>prompt engineering</1> — changing <2>how we ask</2> the question. Head to the next tab to see how.
categories.oltpDesc: High IOPS, tiny blocks, sub-ms latency
categories.vdiDesc: Mixed R/W, small blocks, many users
stop.promptBasic.concept.heading: Prompt Engineering: How you ask matters
stop.promptBasic.concept.p1: Imagine you're asking a new colleague for help. If you say "Hey, what do you think about this?" and hand them a spreadsheet, you'll get a vague answer. But if you say "I need you to categorize this into one of six buckets — here are the buckets, here's the format I want — go," you'll get exactly what you need.
stop.promptBasic.concept.p2: Prompt engineering is the same idea applied to language models. The model doesn't change — the weights stay frozen, the architecture is the same. You're just changing the <1>input text</1> to guide the model toward the output you want.
stop.promptBasic.concept.keyInsight: <1>Key insight:</1> Prompt engineering does not modify the model. There is no training, no new data, no GPU time. You are only changing the text the model receives as input. Think of it as writing better instructions on the exam paper — the student is the same, but clearer questions produce clearer answers.
stop.promptBasic.variant.vagueVerdict: The model has no idea what we actually want. It explains the acronyms like a dictionary.
stop.promptBasic.variant.structuredVerdi: Much better! Giving it categories and a role narrows its focus. But it hedges with "appears to be."
stop.promptBasic.variant.instructionVerd: Tight instructions produce tight output. The model follows the exact format we asked for.
stop.promptBasic.concept.whatChanged: What changed across those three prompts?
stop.promptBasic.concept.vagueExplain: No role, no categories, no format. The model treats it like a general knowledge question and explains what the acronyms mean.
stop.promptBasic.concept.structuredExpla: We give the model a role ("storage I/O expert"), list the six categories, and provide the metrics clearly. The model now attempts classification but still hedges its language.
stop.promptBasic.concept.instructionExpl: We specify the exact output format: category name, confidence, one sentence. The model follows instructions precisely and produces a clean, actionable answer.
stop.promptBasic.concept.limitationsTeas: Prompt engineering is powerful — and it's free. No training, no GPUs, no data pipelines. But it has limits: the model still doesn't truly <1>understand</1> storage I/O patterns. It's following instructions, not applying learned expertise. We'll explore those limits in later stops.
stop.promptBasic.demo.whatModelSeesP: Language models don't just receive your question — they receive a structured prompt. Here's the full text we send to SmolLM2-360M:
stop.promptBasic.demo.resultP1: Even with a system message and a clear instruction, the base model fails to classify. It describes the metrics accurately but never commits to a category. The response ends with "could be from various types of storage workloads" — which is not useful.
stop.promptBasic.demo.resultP2: The correct answer is <1>OLTP Database</1>. The pattern — high IOPS, sub-ms latency, small random blocks, read-heavy — is textbook OLTP. But the model has no training data that maps these patterns to workload categories, so prompting alone isn't enough.
stop.promptBasic.demo.canDoList: Control output format (JSON, bullet points, single word)
Assign a role to focus the model's behavior
Provide categories to constrain possible answers
Include examples (few-shot) for the model to mimic
stop.promptBasic.demo.cannotDoList: Teach the model new domain knowledge
Make the model reliably distinguish similar categories
Replace training when the task requires specialized reasoning
stop.promptBasic.deepdive.tokenProbsP1: Every time a language model generates a word (or more precisely, a <1>token</1>), it isn't just picking one answer. It's computing a probability distribution over its entire vocabulary — tens of thousands of possible next tokens — and then sampling from that distribution.
stop.promptBasic.deepdive.tokenProbsP2: The chart below shows the model's probability distribution for the <1>very first token</1> of its response. This is the moment of truth: what does the model think it should say first?
stop.promptBasic.deepdive.chart1: <1>The top tokens are generic words.</1> "This" (18%), "The" (14%), "Based" (9%). The model's strongest instinct is to start a descriptive sentence — not to classify.
stop.promptBasic.deepdive.chart2: <1>"OLTP" has only 4% probability</1> (highlighted in green). The correct answer is in the model's vocabulary — it's not impossible for the model to say it — but it's buried under a pile of generic alternatives.
stop.promptBasic.deepdive.chart3: <1>The distribution is flat.</1> No single token dominates. This is what uncertainty looks like in a language model — it has no strong opinion about how to start, so probability is spread thinly across many options.
stop.promptBasic.deepdive.whyThisMatters: When we later apply fine-tuning techniques (SFT, DPO, GRPO), the goal is to reshape this probability distribution. A well-trained model for this task should put 80%+ probability on "OLTP" and near-zero on generic words like "This" or "The."

That's the core idea of post-training: <1>shifting the model's probability distribution</1> so that the correct, domain-specific answer becomes the most likely output — not just a long-shot possibility buried at 4%.
stop.promptBasic.deepdive.whatIsATokenP: Language models don't work with whole words. They split text into "tokens" — subword pieces. "OLTP" might be one token, "Database" another. SmolLM2-360M has a vocabulary of ~49,152 tokens. Every generation step picks from this full set based on the probability distribution.
stop.promptBasic.deepdive.tempAndSamplin: The probabilities shown are the model's raw "logits" after softmax. In practice, a "temperature" parameter can sharpen or flatten this distribution before sampling. Lower temperature = more deterministic (picks the top token more often). But even at temperature 0, a flat distribution like this produces unreliable output because "This" and "The" are so close in probability.
stop.promptFewShot.heading: What if we showed the model some examples first?
stop.promptFewShot.introP1: What you just saw was <1>zero-shot</1> prompting — instructions only, no examples. The model knew what we wanted but had never seen a correct answer. Now we try <2>few-shot</2> prompting: we prepend a handful of labeled examples directly into the prompt so the model can mimic the pattern. The naming is literal — zero examples, one example, or a few.
stop.promptFewShot.introP2: Below are three input → output pairs we inject before the real question. The model sees them and learns the expected format on the fly — no training required, just a longer prompt.
stop.promptFewShot.examplesNote: These examples are prepended to the prompt:
stop.promptFewShot.resultP: With few-shot examples, the model follows the format and gets the right answer. But it needed 3 examples eating up your context window to get here.
stop.promptFewShot.probsExplain: Compare the first-token probabilities. Without examples, the model spreads probability across generic words. With few-shot examples, "Classification" jumps to 42% — the model learned the format. But "OLTP" is still only at 18%. It's better, but not confident.
stop.promptFewShot.probsLabel: First Token: Basic Prompt vs Few-Shot
stop.promptLimitation.heading: Where prompting breaks down
stop.promptLimitation.introP1: Few-shot prompting worked well on clear-cut patterns. But real-world storage workloads aren't always obvious — some patterns sit right on the boundary between two categories. Below is an I/O profile that could plausibly be either OLTP or VDI. Let's see how the model handles ambiguity.
stop.promptLimitation.introP2: This is the fundamental ceiling of prompt engineering: no matter how well you phrase the question, you can't give the model expertise it doesn't have.
stop.promptLimitation.ambiguousHint: This pattern blurs the line between OLTP Database and VDI. Correct answer: VDI Virtual Desktop.
stop.promptLimitation.problem1P: The model got it wrong — this is VDI, not OLTP. The balanced read/write ratio (55/45) and high queue depth (64) are VDI indicators, but the model fixated on block size.
stop.promptLimitation.problem2P: Even when it gets answers right, the model hedges: "could potentially be", "I should note". Your ops team needs a clear answer, not uncertainty.
stop.promptLimitation.problem3P: Those few-shot examples disappear after every conversation. You're spending context window tokens teaching the model something it should just <1>know</1>.
stop.promptLimitation.transition: <1>The ceiling of prompting:</1> You can make the model better by giving it more context, but you can't make it fundamentally smarter or change its behavior. For that, we need to move up the map to <2>RAG</2> — or across to <3>Post Training</3>.
stop.ragSimple.problem.heading: Few-shot prompting has limits
stop.ragSimple.problem.intro: In the previous stop, we added examples to the prompt and the model improved. But there are real problems with this approach:
stop.ragSimple.problem.contextFiniteP: Every example you add consumes tokens. With 6 workload categories and multiple examples each, you'd use thousands of tokens just for examples — leaving less room for the actual query and response.
stop.ragSimple.problem.contextRotP: Language models process everything — system prompt, conversation history, RAG documents, few-shot examples, and their own response — inside a single fixed-size <1>context window</1>. When that window fills up, older content gets dropped or summarized, leading to <2>context rot</2>: the model quietly forgets instructions, contradicts earlier answers, or misses key details buried in the middle of long inputs.
stop.ragSimple.problem.contextDeepDive: Deep dive: Context windows, rot, and management strategies →
stop.ragSimple.problem.staticP: The same examples appear every time, regardless of what the user is asking about. If they ask about a backup pattern, the OLTP examples are wasted context.
stop.ragSimple.problem.cantScaleP: Your organization has hundreds of documented patterns, runbooks, and vendor specs. You can't fit all that into a prompt.
stop.ragSimple.problem.whatIfLookUp: <1>What if the model could look things up?</1> Instead of cramming everything into the prompt, what if the model had access to a reference library — and could search it for just the relevant information before answering?
stop.ragSimple.concept.ragP: RAG adds a "lookup" step before the model generates a response. Instead of relying only on what's in the prompt or what the model memorized during training, RAG <1>searches a knowledge base</1> for information relevant to the current query and includes it in the prompt automatically.
stop.ragSimple.concept.embeddingsP: Text is converted into a list of numbers (a "vector") that captures its meaning. Similar texts produce similar vectors. This is done by an embedding model — a separate, small neural network specialized for this task.
stop.ragSimple.concept.vectorSearchP: Your query is embedded into the same vector space as the knowledge base. Then we find the nearest neighbors — the entries whose vectors are most similar to your query's vector. This is "semantic search": it finds relevant content even if the exact words don't match.
stop.ragSimple.concept.keyInsight: <1>Key insight:</1> RAG does not change the model's weights at all. The model is identical — only the input it receives is different. RAG changes what the model <2>sees</2>, not how it <3>thinks</3>.
stop.ragSimple.demo.step2: Step 2: Model responds with retrieved context
stop.ragSimple.demo.resultP: With retrieved context, the model nails it — correct classification, cites the reference patterns, and explains its reasoning using data from the knowledge base.
stop.ragSimple.deepdive.heading: How Vector Search Actually Works
stop.ragSimple.deepdive.p: Each entry in the knowledge base has been pre-converted into an embedding vector (hundreds of numbers). When your query arrives, it's also embedded. Then we find which stored vectors are closest to the query vector. Here's what that looks like in 2D (real embeddings have hundreds of dimensions, but the clustering principle is the same):
stop.ragSimple.deepdive.whyClusterP: The embedding model was trained on millions of text pairs, learning that "high IOPS with small blocks" and "transaction processing database" are semantically related — so they get similar vectors. OLTP entries cluster together, backup entries cluster together, etc. This happens automatically from the meaning of the text.
stop.ragSimple.deepdive.infraForVectorP: The embedding vectors are stored in a <1>vector database</1> (e.g., Pinecone, Milvus, pgvector). For our 13-entry demo, an in-memory array works fine. At enterprise scale with millions of entries, you need optimized approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) indexes — and that means dedicated storage and compute for the vector DB.
stop.ragSimple.deepdive.embeddingIndexes: ~1.5 KB per entry (768-dim float32). 1M entries ≈ 1.5 GB.
stop.ragSimple.deepdive.sourceDocumentsP: The original text. Often larger than the embeddings themselves.
stop.ragSimple.deepdive.queryLatencyP: Vector search adds 10-50ms per query. ANN indexes trade accuracy for speed.
stop.ragLimitation.heading: RAG gave the model knowledge. It didn't give it discipline.
stop.ragLimitation.introP1: In the previous stop, RAG worked beautifully — the model pulled the right reference pattern and classified correctly. But that was a clear-cut case. Now we give it the same ambiguous I/O pattern that tripped up few-shot prompting and see whether having a knowledge base is enough.
stop.ragLimitation.introP2: Spoiler: the model gets the right answer this time. The problem is <1>how</1> it delivers it.
stop.ragLimitation.inputLabel: Ambiguous I/O Pattern (same one that tripped up few-shot)
stop.ragLimitation.rightAnswerP: The model eventually gets to VDI, but buried it in 6 paragraphs of hedging. Your ops team doesn't have time to read a dissertation on every classification.
stop.ragLimitation.behaviorVsKnowledgeP: RAG solved the <1>knowledge</1> problem — the model has the right reference patterns. But it didn't solve the <2>behavior</2> problem — the model doesn't know your team's preferred format, confidence level, or conciseness.
stop.ragLimitation.transition: <1>The ceiling of RAG:</1> RAG changes what the model <2>sees</2>. But to change how the model <3>behaves</3>— its format, confidence, style — you need to change the model itself. That's what <4>Post Training</4> does. We're crossing from the left side of the map to the right.
stop.sft.problem.heading: Where we left off: RAG got the right answer
stop.sft.problem.p: In the previous stop we added Retrieval-Augmented Generation. The model could look up reference patterns from a knowledge base before answering. It worked — the answers became <1>correct</1>. But there is a catch.
stop.sft.problem.verboseP: The model hedges, qualifies, and over-explains. Your monitoring dashboard cannot parse a five-paragraph essay. It needs a structured three-line response that can be fed directly into an automation pipeline.
stop.sft.problem.wrongFormatP: There is no "Classification:" prefix. No "Confidence:" level. The model structures its output like a general assistant, not like a storage operations tool. RAG gave it the right data but cannot control the output shape.
stop.sft.problem.uncertainToneP: "Could potentially be classified as either..." is not actionable. When the on-call engineer gets a page at 3 AM, they need a clear answer with a confidence level — not a research paper on the topic.
stop.sft.problem.transitionP1: <1>RAG changes what the model sees.</1> But the model's behavior — its format, confidence style, and conciseness — stays the same. To change how the model <2>acts</2>, we need to change the model itself. That means modifying its weights through training.
stop.sft.problem.transitionP2: This is where <1>Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)</1> comes in. We are crossing from the left side of the map (prompt engineering, RAG) to the right side (post-training). From here on, we are changing the model.
stop.sft.problem.techCorrectNote: Technically correct — eventually says "VDI" — but buried in 6 paragraphs of hedging.
stop.sft.problem.desiredLabel: What your ops team actually needs
stop.sft.problem.threeLines: Three lines. Classification, confidence, reasoning. Done.
stop.sft.concept.heading: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)
stop.sft.concept.p1: SFT is the simplest form of post-training. You take a pre-trained language model and show it examples of the behavior you want. The model adjusts its internal weights so that it produces outputs matching your examples. It is "supervised" because every training example has a known correct answer.
stop.sft.concept.analogy: Think of it like training a new employee with an example handbook. You hand them 1,400 examples that say: "When you see this I/O pattern, respond exactly like this." After studying the handbook, they internalize the patterns and can handle new cases they have never seen before.
stop.sft.concept.trainingExampleHeading: What a Training Example Looks Like
stop.sft.concept.trainingExampleP: Each training example is an input-output pair. The input is a raw I/O metric string (what we want the model to classify). The output is the structured response we want the model to produce. Here is one of the 1,400 examples:
stop.sft.concept.datasetNote: The dataset contains 1,400 examples across 6 workload categories (OLTP, OLAP, VDI, Backup, AI/ML Training, Video Streaming) with varying difficulty levels.
stop.sft.concept.loraHeading: LoRA: Training a Patch, Not the Whole Model
stop.sft.concept.loraP: SmolLM2-360M has 360 million parameters. Retraining all of them would require significant GPU memory, time, and storage. <1>LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)</1> is a technique that freezes the entire original model and trains only a small set of adapter weights that get layered on top.
stop.sft.concept.loraAnalogy: Instead of rewriting the entire model (360M parameters), we only train a small adapter (<1>432K parameters — 0.12%</1> of the total). Think of it like applying a firmware patch rather than reflashing the entire image. The base model stays intact; you just overlay a tiny delta that changes the behavior.
stop.sft.concept.loraTargets: LoRA targets the <1>attention projections</1> (q_proj and v_proj) inside each of the model's 32 transformer layers. These are the matrices that control what the model pays attention to and what information it carries forward. By adapting just these projections with a rank-16 decomposition, LoRA captures the essential behavioral change in a tiny number of parameters.
stop.sft.concept.loraAsideToggleShow: How does LoRA select which parameters to train?
stop.sft.concept.loraAsideP: LoRA doesn't search for parameters — you configure which weight matrices to target. The standard choice is the <1>query and value projections</1> (q_proj, v_proj) in each attention layer, because research shows these have the most impact on model behavior. The adapter itself is a pair of small matrices (A and B) whose product approximates the weight change that full fine-tuning would make. The "rank" (16 in our case) controls how expressive the adapter is — higher rank = more capacity, but more parameters.
stop.sft.concept.loraDeepDiveLink: Deep dive: LoRA parameter selection, rank decomposition, and the training loop →
stop.sft.concept.mergingNote: During inference, the adapter weights are merged with the base model at near-zero cost. You can even swap different adapters for different tasks on the same base model — like hot-swapping firmware modules.
stop.sft.concept.keyInsightP: <1>Key insight:</1> SFT changes the model's weights. After training, the model permanently knows how to classify I/O patterns — no prompt engineering needed. You don't need to tell it the format, the categories, or the reasoning style every time. It learned all of that from the 1,400 examples. The adapter is a 1.7 MB file that gets merged at load time, and the model behaves differently from that point forward.
stop.sft.demo.whatChangedP1: <1>What changed?</1> The base model sees the same metrics and produces vague, hedging descriptions. It never commits to a classification, never uses a structured format, and often gets the category wrong entirely.
stop.sft.demo.whatChangedP2: After SFT, the model has learned three things: <1>the format</1> (Classification / Confidence / Key indicators), <2>the task</2> (commit to a specific workload category), and <3>the reasoning style</3> (cite the specific metrics that led to the decision). All from 1,400 examples and {{minutes}} minutes of training.
stop.sft.deepdive.howSftShifts: How SFT Shifts the Model's Confidence
stop.sft.deepdive.howSftShiftsP: A language model generates text one token at a time. At each step, it assigns a probability to every possible next token. The chart below shows the probability distribution for the <1>very first token</1> the model generates. Before SFT, probability is spread across generic words ("This", "The", "Based"). After SFT, probability mass concentrates on structured tokens like "Classification".
stop.sft.deepdive.probsLabel: First Token: Base Model vs SFT
stop.sft.deepdive.realTokenProbs: Using real token probabilities from trained model.
stop.sft.deepdive.actualWeightChangesP: This heatmap shows the LoRA weight delta for one attention layer. Each cell represents how much that specific weight was adjusted during training. The structured patterns — not random noise — confirm the model learned meaningful features rather than memorizing individual examples.
stop.sft.deepdive.attentionLayerP: Language models are built from <1>Transformers</1> — a stack of identical layers (SmolLM2 has 32). Each layer contains an <2>attention mechanism</2> that lets every token in the input "look at" every other token to decide what's relevant. This is how the model connects "IOPS: 45000" with "OLTP Database" even when they're far apart. The attention mechanism uses learned weight matrices (Q, K, V) — and that's exactly where LoRA inserts its small trainable adapters.
stop.sft.deepdive.transformerDeepDiveLin: Deep dive: Transformers, attention, and how LoRA fits in →
stop.sft.deepdive.trainingProgressP: The loss curve shows how the model improved over {{steps}} optimization steps. <1>Loss</1> measures how far the model's output is from the desired output — lower is better.
stop.sft.deepdive.realTrainingLoss: Real training loss from actual SFT run.
stop.sft.deepdive.epochBoundariesP: Small bumps at epoch boundaries are normal — the model sees the data in a new random order each epoch.
stop.sft.deepdive.finalLossP: The loss converges around {{value}}. Going lower would risk overfitting.
stop.sft.deepdive.storageIOImpactP: Each checkpoint write creates a burst of storage I/O. The training data stream is a steady sequential read.
stop.dpo.problem.heading: SFT taught the model WHAT to say. But not HOW to say it.
stop.dpo.problem.p: After supervised fine-tuning, our SmolLM2-360M model can correctly classify storage I/O patterns. It knows that high IOPS with small blocks and low latency means OLTP. That's the "what." But look at the difference in <1>how</1> two correct answers can sound:
stop.dpo.problem.bothCorrect: Both answers are correct. So why does it matter?
stop.dpo.problem.whyItMattersP1: Imagine it's 3 AM during an incident and your monitoring pipeline surfaces one of these responses. Response A gives you a clear classification, a confidence level, and the reasoning in a scannable format. Response B waffles, hedges, and buries the answer in qualifications. In production, that difference matters.
stop.dpo.problem.whyItMattersP2: The model defaults to the verbose, hedging style because cautious language was more common in its pretraining data. Phrases like "could potentially be" and "I'm not entirely certain" are safe bets when you've been trained on internet text where hedging is everywhere.
stop.dpo.problem.whyItMattersP3: SFT can't fix this because it only trains on "correct vs. incorrect." It has no mechanism to express <1>"correct AND well-formatted"</1> vs. <2>"correct but unhelpful."</2>
stop.dpo.problem.transition: We need a way to teach the model our <1>style preferences</1> — not just what answer to give, but how to give it. That's exactly what <2>Direct Preference Optimization (DPO)</2> does. Head to the next tab to see how it works.
stop.dpo.concept.heading: Teaching with comparisons, not examples
stop.dpo.concept.p1: SFT teaches by showing the model examples: "given this input, produce this output." <1>DPO (Direct Preference Optimization)</1> teaches a different way — through comparisons. Instead of saying "here's the right answer," you say "this answer is <2>better than</2> that one."
stop.dpo.concept.analogy: Think of it like training a new hire. SFT is handing them a style guide and saying "write reports like this." DPO is showing them two reports side by side and saying "this one is better — do you see why?" Both work, but comparisons teach nuances that are hard to spell out in a single example.
stop.dpo.concept.rlhfP: Before DPO, the standard approach to preference learning was <1>Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF)</1>. It works, but it's complicated and expensive:
stop.dpo.concept.rlhfStep1: <1>Collect preferences.</1> Show humans two model outputs for the same input. They pick the better one. (This is exactly what you'll do in the "See It Work" tab.)
stop.dpo.concept.rlhfStep2: <1>Train a reward model.</1> A separate neural network learns to predict which response a human would prefer. That's Model #2 in GPU memory.
stop.dpo.concept.rlhfStep3: <1>Optimize with PPO.</1> Use Proximal Policy Optimization (a reinforcement learning algorithm) to maximize the reward model's score. This requires a value network too — Model #3 in memory.
stop.dpo.concept.rlhfCost: Three models running simultaneously. Unstable training dynamics. 45 minutes for our small 360M model. It works, but the engineering cost is steep.
stop.dpo.concept.dpoSkipHeading: DPO: skip the reward model entirely
stop.dpo.concept.dpoSkipP: In 2023, Rafailov et al. published a key insight: there is a <1>closed-form solution</1> that maps directly from preference data to the optimal policy. You don't need to train a reward model first and then do RL against it. You can collapse those two steps into a single training loss.
stop.dpo.concept.rlhfCostLine: 12.8 GB GPU memory. 45 min training. Unstable.
stop.dpo.concept.dpoCostLine: 5.1 GB GPU memory. {{minutes}} min training. Stable.
stop.dpo.concept.rlhfAnalogy: <1>Analogy:</1> RLHF is like hiring a quality inspector (the reward model) to check every report your employee writes, then giving the employee feedback based on the inspector's scores. DPO is like the employee learning directly from your feedback — no middleman. Same outcome, less overhead.
stop.dpo.concept.dpoLossPlain: <1>In plain English:</1> For each preference pair, compare how likely the model thinks the chosen response is vs. the rejected one. If the model already agrees with the human preference, the loss is low. If it disagrees, the loss is high and the gradient pushes the model to increase the probability of the chosen response and decrease the rejected one.
stop.dpo.concept.betaExplain: <1>beta</1> controls how aggressively preferences are enforced. A high beta means "stick closely to the reference model, just nudge preferences." A low beta means "reshape outputs dramatically." We used beta = 0.1 for our training run.
stop.dpo.concept.keyInsight: <1>Key insight:</1> DPO doesn't need a separate reward model. It learns preferences directly from chosen/rejected pairs — same result as RLHF with 60% less compute. For our demo: 400 preference pairs, 0.12% of parameters trained via LoRA, {{minutes}} minutes of training on a single GPU.
stop.dpo.demo.intro: You're about to do exactly what DPO training data looks like: comparing two model outputs and choosing the one you prefer. We have <1>3 preference pairs</1> below. Both responses in each pair are <2>correct</2> classifications — the difference is in style, confidence, and helpfulness. Click the one you'd rather see in a production monitoring system.
stop.dpo.demo.bothCorrectChoose: Both responses are correct. Which one do you prefer? Click to choose.
stop.dpo.demo.matchesTraining: That matches the training data's preference.
stop.dpo.demo.trainingPreferred: Interesting! The training data preferred Response {{choice}}, but preferences are subjective — that's the whole point.
stop.dpo.demo.ambiguous: This one is genuinely ambiguous — reasonable people disagree, and that's informative too.
stop.dpo.demo.whyBLessPreferred: Why B is typically less preferred:
stop.dpo.demo.dpoExplanation: You just did exactly what DPO training data looks like: comparing two outputs and saying which is better. 400 pairs like this is all it takes to reshape the model's style.
stop.dpo.deepdive.heading: How DPO reshapes the model's probabilities
stop.dpo.deepdive.logProbShiftTitle: Log Probability Shift: Before vs After DPO
stop.dpo.deepdive.beforeP: <1>Before DPO</1>, the model actually slightly preferred the verbose response (log prob -1.8 vs. -2.1 for concise). Hedging language was more common in its training data, so the model defaulted to "cautious" phrasing.
stop.dpo.deepdive.afterP: <1>After DPO</1>, the concise response jumped to -0.9 (strongly preferred) while the verbose response dropped to -3.2 (strongly suppressed). That's a complete reversal — the model now generates the concise style by default.
stop.dpo.deepdive.gapP: <1>The gap matters.</1> Before DPO the difference was only 0.3 (almost a coin flip). After DPO it's 2.3 — the model is now highly confident about which style to use. Your 400 preference pairs literally reshaped the model's writing style.
stop.dpo.deepdive.insightP: Increase log probability of the chosen response, decrease the rejected. No intermediate reward model needed. Beta (0.1 in our run) controls how aggressively preferences are enforced.
stop.dpo.deepdive.rlhfSteps: Train a separate <1>reward model</1> on those preferences
Use PPO to optimize the policy against the reward model
Need a <1>value network</1> for PPO baseline
stop.dpo.deepdive.rlhfCost: 3 models in memory simultaneously. 45 minutes training.
stop.dpo.deepdive.dpoSteps: No reward model, no PPO, no value network
Math proves this is equivalent to implicit reward modeling
stop.dpo.deepdive.dpoCost: 1 model in memory. {{minutes}} minutes training. Same result.
stop.grpo.problem.heading: DPO improved style, but humans are still in the loop
stop.grpo.problem.p: DPO was a big step forward: it removed the reward model and simplified the training pipeline. But it still required <1>400 human-labeled preference pairs</1>. Someone had to look at output pairs and decide which was better. For our storage I/O classifier, that meant an engineer spending hours reviewing outputs, comparing styles, and clicking "this one is better."
stop.grpo.problem.insightHeading: What if the answer itself could be the teacher?
stop.grpo.problem.insightP1: For many tasks, there is a clear right answer. Our storage I/O classification is one of them: "OLAP Analytics" is either the correct label or it isn't. We don't need a human to tell us that — we can check programmatically. Math problems, code correctness, factual lookups — all of these have <1>verifiable answers</1>.
stop.grpo.problem.insightP2: This is the frontier of post-training: <1>Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR)</1>. Instead of collecting human preferences, we let the model generate multiple attempts, check which ones are correct, and use that signal to improve. The algorithm that makes this work is called <2>GRPO</2> — Group Relative Policy Optimization — and it's the technique behind DeepSeek R1.
stop.grpo.problem.transition: <1>Can the model learn from its own attempts?</1> That's GRPO. Head to the next tab to see how it works.
stop.grpo.concept.heading: Group Relative Policy Optimization
stop.grpo.concept.subtitle: The technique behind DeepSeek R1.
stop.grpo.concept.p: GRPO takes a radically different approach from both RLHF and DPO. Instead of collecting preferences from humans, the model <1>generates multiple attempts</1> for the same input, <2>scores them with a verifiable reward</2> (did it get the right answer?), and uses the <3>group statistics as a baseline</3> to figure out which attempts were above-average and which were below.
stop.grpo.concept.step1P: Given one input prompt, the model generates <1>8 different completions</1>. Each might classify the I/O pattern differently, with varying confidence and reasoning.
stop.grpo.concept.step2P: Each completion is scored: <1>correct classification = 1</1>, <2>wrong = 0</2>. No reward model, no human labeler. The answer key is the teacher.
stop.grpo.concept.step3P: The average reward across all 8 generations becomes the <1>baseline</1>. No separate critic network or reward model needed — just simple group statistics.
stop.grpo.concept.step4P: Compare each generation's reward to the group average. <1>Above average = reinforce.</1> <2>Below average = suppress.</2>
stop.grpo.concept.keyDifference: <1>Key difference from PPO:</1> Traditional RLHF with PPO requires a separately-trained critic network and a separate reward model — both expensive to train and maintain. GRPO replaces both with <2>group statistics</2>: just compute the mean and standard deviation of the group's rewards. This means roughly <3>50% less compute</3> compared to PPO, with no extra models to train.
stop.grpo.concept.formulaPlain: <1>In plain English:</1> For each generation in the group, subtract the group's mean reward. If you got a reward of 1.0 and the group mean is 0.5, your advantage is positive — you did better than average. If you got 0.0, your advantage is negative — you did worse. Dividing by standard deviation normalizes the scores so the model makes meaningful gradient updates.
stop.grpo.concept.positiveAdvantageP: → increase the probability of generating this response.
stop.grpo.concept.negativeAdvantageP: → decrease the probability of generating this response.
stop.grpo.concept.studyGroupAnalogy: <1>Analogy:</1> Imagine a study group where everyone answers the same exam question, then they grade each other's homework. The answer key is the teacher — no human grader needed. Students who scored above the group average get encouragement ("do more of this"), and those who scored below get correction ("do less of this"). Over time, the whole group converges on consistently right answers.
stop.grpo.concept.keyInsight: <1>Key insight:</1> GRPO needs no human labels and no reward model. The answer itself is the teacher. For any task with a verifiable correct answer — classification, math, code — the model can improve itself purely from its own attempts.
stop.grpo.concept.grpoLongerNote: GRPO takes longer per run because it generates 8 completions per prompt (8x the compute per step). But it eliminates the hours of human labeling that DPO requires, making it far more scalable to new tasks.
stop.grpo.concept.rlvrHeading: RLVR: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards
stop.grpo.concept.rlvrP: GRPO is powerful, but it only works when you can <1>automatically check</1> if an answer is correct. This class of problems is called <2>Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR)</2>. Examples:
stop.grpo.concept.rlvrOurTask: <1>Our task:</1> Does "Classification: OLAP Analytics" match the ground truth label? Verifiable.
stop.grpo.concept.rlvrMath: <1>Math:</1> Does the model's answer equal 42? Verifiable.
stop.grpo.concept.rlvrCode: <1>Code:</1> Do the unit tests pass? Verifiable.
stop.grpo.concept.rlvrCreative: <1>Creative writing:</1> Is this poem "good"? <2>Not</2> verifiable — you'd still need DPO or RLHF for this.
stop.grpo.concept.rlvrPractical: RLVR is what makes GRPO practical. For any task with a clear right/wrong answer, you can skip human labeling entirely.
stop.grpo.demo.intro: Below is an ambiguous I/O pattern that could plausibly be OLAP Analytics or AI/ML Training. The model generates <1>8 attempts</1>. Each is automatically scored: correct classification = 1.0, incorrect = 0.0. Reveal them one at a time or all at once to see how the group splits.
stop.grpo.demo.challengeInput: Challenge Input (Ambiguous: OLAP vs AI Training)
stop.grpo.demo.modelGenerates: The model generates <1>{{count}} attempts</1>. Each is scored: correct = 1.0, incorrect = 0.0.
stop.grpo.demo.clickToReveal: Click "Reveal next" to see this generation...
stop.grpo.demo.howGrpoLearns: <1>How GRPO learns:</1> The mean reward ({{mean}}) is the baseline. The {{correct}} correct generations get <2>positive advantage</2> (+{{posAdv}}) — they're reinforced. The {{incorrect}} incorrect generations get <3>negative advantage</3> ({{negAdv}}) — they're suppressed. No critic network, no reward model — just group statistics.
stop.grpo.demo.deepseekNote: This is the technique behind DeepSeek R1. The answer itself is the teacher. For our task, "correct classification" is verifiable. For math, "correct answer" is verifiable. That's why it's called Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR).
stop.grpo.deepdive.advantageP: This is the core GRPO mechanism. The group mean reward (0.50) is the baseline. Correct classifications (green) get <1>positive advantage</1> — they're reinforced. Incorrect ones (red) get <2>negative advantage</2> — they're suppressed. The model learns to generate more responses like the winners.
stop.grpo.deepdive.advantageNote: Notice: no external reward model, no human labels. The advantage is computed entirely from the group's own statistics. This is what makes GRPO 50% cheaper than PPO.
stop.grpo.deepdive.accuracyP: Classification accuracy improves over training as the model learns from its own successes and failures. Starting at ~45% (random-ish), reaching ~85% after 200 steps. Each step involves generating 8 completions, scoring them, and updating the model toward the correct ones.
stop.grpo.deepdive.rewardP: Mean group reward increases as more generations become correct. This curve directly reflects the model's improving ability to classify I/O patterns. A mean reward of 1.0 would mean every generation in every group is correct.
stop.grpo.deepdive.updateRuleP: <1>Reading the formula:</1> The advantage tells the model how much better (or worse) each generation was compared to the group. Multiplying by log_prob means "adjust the probability of generating this response by this much." Positive advantage = reinforce. Negative = suppress. The std normalization prevents the model from only making tiny updates.
stop.combined.fullJourneyP: Each technique built on the last. Prompting gave format. RAG gave knowledge. SFT gave classification skill. DPO gave style. GRPO gave reasoning precision. The combination in the upper-right quadrant is greater than the sum of its parts.
stop.infra.dataCuration.heading: The Real Bottleneck: Data Curation
stop.infra.dataCuration.p: At 360M parameters, all three techniques train in under 35 minutes on a single GPU. The real bottleneck at this scale isn't compute — it's <1>data curation</1>: labeling 1,400 examples for SFT, creating preference pairs for DPO, designing reward functions for GRPO. At 7B–70B scale, training becomes multi-hour to multi-day GPU jobs where storage throughput and checkpoint I/O dominate.
stop.infra.scaling.heading: Scaling: Checkpoint Storage by Model Size
stop.infra.takeaway.checkpointIOP: LoRA adapters are {{pct}}% smaller than full model checkpoints. At 70B scale, that's 340 MB vs 140 GB per save — the difference between milliseconds and minutes on network storage.
stop.infra.takeaway.readPatternsP: Base model load is the dominant read: {{size}} MB sequential. Training datasets are comparatively tiny. DPO and GRPO read the base model + SFT adapter, creating a dependency chain on storage throughput.
stop.infra.takeaway.grpoBurstP: GRPO generates 8 completions per prompt — 90% of wall-clock time is GPU compute, not storage I/O. But checkpoint saves create write bursts. On shared storage, these bursts can cause contention with other training jobs.
stop.infra.scalingRealityP: At demo scale (360M params), GPU time is trivial — the human effort of data curation dwarfs compute cost. At production scale (7B–70B), the equation flips: multi-day training runs make storage throughput, checkpoint frequency, and network bandwidth the dominant infrastructure concerns. Plan for both bottlenecks.

stop.epilogue.ainosP1: Everything in this explorer required manual decisions — which technique to try, what hyperparameters to use, when to stop training. But what if an AI system could analyze your results and propose the next experiment?

It amplifies engineer judgment rather than replacing it: the AI proposes, you approve, the system executes. The infrastructure implications for storage and compute teams are significant — automated experiment loops mean predictable, bursty GPU demand and structured artifact management at scale.

deepdive.transformers.title: Transformers and Attention: The Architecture Behind LLMs
deepdive.transformers.subtitle: Every large language model — GPT, Claude, Llama, SmolLM2 — is built on the Transformer architecture. Explore how it works interactively.
deepdive.transformers.positionalEncoding: + positional encoding (where each token is in the sequence)
deepdive.transformers.selfAttention: Self-Attention — "which other tokens should I pay attention to?"
deepdive.transformers.ffn: Feed-Forward Network — process each token independently
deepdive.transformers.layerRepeat: This block repeats 32 times. Each is called a "layer."
deepdive.transformers.outputDesc: Probability distribution over ~49,152 possible next tokens
deepdive.transformers.layersBuildP: Different layers capture different levels of abstraction:
deepdive.transformers.earlyLayers: Surface patterns: token identity, position, basic syntax
deepdive.transformers.middleLayers: Semantic meaning: "45000 IOPS is high", number-to-concept mapping
deepdive.transformers.lateLayers: Task-level reasoning: combining evidence → "this is OLTP"
deepdive.transformers.multiHeadHeading: 15 attention heads per layer — different perspectives
deepdive.transformers.syntaxHeadsP: Track structural patterns — colons, delimiters, output format.
deepdive.transformers.valueLinkingHeadsP: Connect numbers to their metric labels — "45000" → "IOPS".
deepdive.transformers.crossMetricHeadsP: Combine evidence across IOPS, latency, and block size for classification.
deepdive.transformers.exploreInteractive: Explore these patterns interactively in the <1>Attention Explorer</1> tab.
deepdive.context.title: The Context Window: What It Is, Why It Breaks, and How to Manage It
deepdive.context.subtitle: Every interaction with a language model happens inside a fixed-size window of text. Understanding how that window works — and fails — is essential to building reliable AI systems.
deepdive.context.whatIsP: A context window is the total amount of text a model can "see" at once — your system prompt, the conversation history, any injected documents (like RAG results), and the model's own response, all concatenated into a single sequence of tokens.
deepdive.context.totalFit: Total: all of the above must fit within the model's token limit
deepdive.context.howBuilt: How context is built and managed
deepdive.context.howBuiltP: The context window isn't just a text buffer — it's actively managed by the application layer. Each component competes for space:
deepdive.context.systemPromptP: Fixed instructions that define the model's role and behavior. Usually 200–1,000 tokens. Set once per conversation and always occupies the same space.
deepdive.context.ragInjectionP: Retrieved documents are inserted before the user's query. The more documents you retrieve (higher top-K), the more tokens consumed. A typical RAG setup might inject 500–2,000 tokens of context per query.
deepdive.context.conversationP: Every prior message — user and assistant — stays in the window. This is what gives the model "memory" within a session. But it grows with every exchange, eventually crowding out everything else.
deepdive.context.fewShotP: Examples injected to demonstrate the desired format. Each example might be 100–300 tokens. Three examples = 300–900 tokens consumed before the model even sees the real query.
deepdive.context.contextRot: Context rot: when more context makes things worse
deepdive.context.contextRotP: Intuitively, more context should help. In practice, it often hurts. As the context window fills up, models exhibit several failure modes:
deepdive.context.lostInMiddleP: Research shows that models pay the most attention to the <1>beginning</1> and <2>end</2> of the context window. Information placed in the middle is disproportionately ignored. If your most relevant RAG document lands in the middle of 10 retrieved passages, the model may miss it entirely.
deepdive.context.attentionDilutionP: The self-attention mechanism distributes "attention" across all tokens in the window. As the window grows, each token gets a thinner slice of attention. Important details that were prominent in a short context become noise in a long one. This is why a concise, well-structured prompt often outperforms a verbose one with more information.
deepdive.context.contradictoryP: Long conversations accumulate stale information. If the user corrected themselves three messages ago, both the wrong and right answers are in the window. The model must figure out which is current — and it doesn't always get it right. RAG can make this worse by retrieving documents that contradict each other.
deepdive.context.slidingWindowP: Drop the oldest messages when the window fills up. Simple but lossy — the model forgets early instructions and context. Works for casual chat, dangerous for multi-step tasks.
deepdive.context.summarizationP: Use the model itself (or a smaller model) to summarize older messages before discarding them. Preserves key facts in fewer tokens. But summaries are lossy too — the model decides what's "important," and it may be wrong.
deepdive.context.structuredMemoryP: Maintain a separate structured store (key-value pairs, task state, user preferences) that gets injected into the system prompt each turn. Only the current state is in the window — not the full history of how it got there.
deepdive.context.ragAsReliefP: Instead of keeping everything in the window, store knowledge externally and retrieve only what's needed per query. This is one of RAG's underappreciated benefits: it lets you keep the window lean while still having access to a large knowledge base.
deepdive.context.postTrainingP1: Context management is an inference-time problem — you're engineering around the model's limitations. Post-training attacks the problem from the other direction: if the model <1>already knows</1> your domain, you need less context to get a good answer.
deepdive.context.postTrainingP2: A fine-tuned model doesn't need few-shot examples (it learned the format during training). It may need fewer RAG documents (it has internalized the domain knowledge). The context window is still finite, but a well-trained model uses it more efficiently.
deepdive.lora.title: LoRA: How It Decides What to Train
deepdive.lora.subtitle: LoRA trains less than 0.12% of a model's parameters and yet changes its behavior dramatically. How does it know <1>which</1> parameters to target, and why does such a small change have such a big effect?
deepdive.lora.whyNotWhole: Why not train the whole model?
deepdive.lora.whyNotWholeP: Full fine-tuning updates every one of the model's 360 million parameters. This requires storing a complete copy of the model in GPU memory (for gradients and optimizer states), which triples the memory footprint. For SmolLM2 that's manageable, but for a 70B-parameter model it's prohibitive.
deepdive.lora.whichParamsP: LoRA doesn't randomly pick parameters. It targets specific <1>weight matrices inside the attention mechanism</1> of each transformer layer. Every attention layer has four key projections:
deepdive.lora.qProjExplain: Controls what each token <1>searches for</1>. Adapting this changes which relationships the model considers important — e.g., learning that "IOPS" and "Latency" together are more informative than either alone.
deepdive.lora.vProjExplain: Controls what <1>information flows forward</1> from each token. Adapting this changes what the model extracts — e.g., learning to carry "45000 = high" rather than just the raw number.
deepdive.lora.whyNotAllFour: <1>Why not all four projections?</1> You can — and sometimes it helps. But each additional target doubles the adapter size. For our task (6-class classification with clear patterns), q_proj + v_proj is enough. For more complex tasks, you might target all four, or even include the feed-forward layers.
deepdive.lora.rankDecomp: Rank decomposition: why it's so small
deepdive.lora.rankDecompP: A normal weight matrix in SmolLM2's attention is 960 x 960 = 921,600 parameters. LoRA replaces the update to this matrix with two much smaller matrices:
deepdive.lora.rank4P: Very few parameters. Fast training, tiny adapter. Works for simple behavioral changes (output format tweaks).
deepdive.lora.rank16P: Good balance of capacity and efficiency. Enough to learn a 6-class classification task with reasoning. This is the most common default.
deepdive.lora.rank64P: Approaches full fine-tuning capacity. Useful for complex tasks or multilingual adaptation. Larger adapter, slower training, more GPU memory.
deepdive.lora.whySmallWorks: Why does such a small change work?
deepdive.lora.whySmallWorksP: This is the key insight from the original LoRA paper: when you fine-tune a large model on a specific task, the weight changes live in a <1>low-dimensional subspace</1>. In plain English:
deepdive.lora.whySmallWorksAnalogy: The base model already knows how to process language, reason about numbers, and follow instructions. Teaching it to classify storage I/O patterns doesn't require changing everything — it just needs a small, specific adjustment to redirect existing capabilities toward the new task. Like adjusting the steering on a car that already knows how to drive.
deepdive.lora.trainingLoop: The training loop: what actually happens
deepdive.lora.trainingLoopP: Here's what happens for each training example, step by step:
deepdive.lora.step1P: The input ("IOPS: 45000...") flows through all 32 layers. At each attention layer, the computation is: output = input x W_frozen + input x A x B. The frozen weights do the heavy lifting; the adapter nudges the result.
deepdive.lora.step2P: The model's output is compared to the desired output ("Classification: OLTP Database..."). The loss function measures how different they are — token by token, using cross-entropy loss. Lower loss = closer to the desired output.
deepdive.lora.step3P: The loss is backpropagated through the network. But here's the key: gradients are only computed for the A and B matrices (the adapter), <1>not</1> for the frozen W matrices. This is what makes LoRA fast and memory-efficient — you skip gradient computation for 99.88% of the parameters.
deepdive.lora.step4P: The optimizer (AdamW) uses the gradients to adjust A and B slightly, pushing the model's output closer to the desired answer. This repeats for each training example, across 3 epochs (3 full passes through the 1,400 examples).
deepdive.lora.step5P: After training, only the A and B matrices are saved (1.7 MB). To use the fine-tuned model, you load the base model and then apply the adapter on top. You can swap adapters for different tasks without re-downloading the base model.
deepdive.lora.paramCountHeading: Where do the 432K parameters come from?
deepdive.lora.paramCountP: Let's count. For each of the 32 layers, we adapt 2 projections (q_proj, v_proj), each with two low-rank matrices:
deepdive.lora.infraImplications: Why this matters for infrastructure teams
deepdive.lora.infraP1: LoRA's efficiency is what makes fine-tuning practical at enterprise scale. A single accelerator with 8 GB VRAM can fine-tune a 360M-parameter model. A T4 (15 GB) can handle models up to ~7B parameters with LoRA. Without LoRA, you'd need 4x the training VRAM and the training time would increase proportionally.
deepdive.lora.infraP2: From a storage perspective, LoRA also means you can keep one base model on disk (720 MB) and maintain dozens of task-specific adapters (1-2 MB each). Swapping tasks means loading a different adapter file — not a different multi-gigabyte model.
deepdive.modelSize.title: Model Size Comparison
deepdive.modelSize.subtitle: How does model scale affect post-training results?
deepdive.modelSize.whySizeMatters.p1: A model's parameter count directly determines its capacity to learn patterns. More parameters mean more internal "knobs" the model can tune during training, which translates to better ability to distinguish subtle differences between storage I/O workload categories. A 1.7B-parameter model has roughly 4.7x the capacity of a 360M model — and that extra capacity shows up in accuracy, especially on ambiguous patterns where the smaller model struggles.
deepdive.modelSize.whySizeMatters.p2: But bigger models come with real infrastructure costs: more VRAM, longer training times, and larger checkpoints. The question for storage and compute teams is not "which is better?" — it's "where is the cost-accuracy tradeoff right for my use case?"
deepdive.modelSize.infraP1: Moving from 360M to 1.7B parameters has cascading effects on infrastructure. GPU memory requirements jump from ~3.2 GB to ~12.5 GB — meaning you shift from "runs on any GPU" to "needs a mid-range or better card." Training times increase 4-5x across all techniques, which means longer GPU reservations and higher compute costs.
deepdive.modelSize.infraP2: For storage teams, larger models also mean larger checkpoints saved more frequently, higher I/O throughput during data loading, and more intermediate artifacts to manage. The decision of which model size to use is fundamentally a capacity planning question that bridges ML engineering and infrastructure operations.
deepdive.modelSize.headToHead.notice: <1>Notice:</1> The 1.7B model tends to produce more detailed reasoning and is less likely to confuse similar workload categories. On ambiguous patterns (like AI/ML vs Video Streaming), the extra capacity helps the larger model distinguish subtle differences in the metrics.
deepdive.modelSize.scaling.chartTitle: Accuracy by Technique and Model Size
deepdive.modelSize.scaling.chartNote: Across all techniques, the 1.7B model consistently outperforms the 360M model. The gap narrows with GRPO, suggesting that reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards helps smaller models punch above their weight.
deepdive.modelSize.scaling.grpoNote: GRPO on 1.7B takes ~150 minutes — generating 8 completions per prompt with a larger model is compute-intensive. For infrastructure teams, this means planning for sustained GPU reservations and higher I/O throughput during training data loading.
transformer.headSyntaxDesc: This head tracks structural patterns — colons that separate labels from values, pipes that delimit metrics, and positional relationships between tokens.
transformer.headValueLinkDesc: This head connects numeric values back to their metric labels — linking "45000" to "IOPS" and "0.3ms" to "Latency". It learns which numbers belong to which metrics.
transformer.headCrossMetricDesc: This head combines evidence across multiple metrics. When deciding the classification, it attends to IOPS, Latency, and Block Size simultaneously — looking at the full picture.
transformer.layerEarlyDesc: Surface patterns — local position, token adjacency
transformer.layerMiddleDesc: Semantic patterns — connecting meaning across tokens
transformer.layerLateDesc: Task reasoning — combining evidence for classification
transformer.insight.syntax_5: The first colon after "workload" — this head recognizes it as the boundary between the task instruction and the data fields.
transformer.insight.syntax_7: Colon after "IOPS" — the syntax head sees this as a label:value separator and uses it to track the structure of the input.
transformer.insight.syntax_10: The pipe character "|" — recognized as a field delimiter, helping the model understand where one metric ends and another begins.
transformer.insight.syntax_17: The final colon — this is the output trigger. The syntax head pays heavy attention here because it signals "now generate the classification."
transformer.insight.valueLink_8: "45" attends strongly back to "IOPS" — the value-link head has learned that this number belongs to the IOPS metric.
transformer.insight.valueLink_9: "000" attends to both "IOPS" and "45" — completing the number 45000 and linking it to its metric label.
transformer.insight.valueLink_13: "0.3" attends strongly to "Latency" — the model connects this sub-millisecond value to the latency metric, which is a key OLTP indicator.
transformer.insight.valueLink_14: "ms" reinforces the Latency connection — the unit confirms this is a time measurement, strengthening the link.
transformer.insight.crossMetric_6: From "IOPS", attention spreads to other metric values — the cross-metric head is already comparing IOPS against latency and block size.
transformer.insight.crossMetric_8: From "45", the model looks at latency (0.3ms) and block size — combining high IOPS + low latency = classic OLTP pattern.
transformer.insight.crossMetric_11: From "Latency", attention reaches back to IOPS values and forward to block size — building a holistic view of the workload.
transformer.insight.crossMetric_17: The final token is the decision point — it gathers evidence from ALL metrics simultaneously: high IOPS (45000), low latency (0.3ms), and block size, then passes this combined signal to generate "OLTP".
forwardPass.tokenize.title: Tokenization
forwardPass.tokenize.subtitle: Text → Tokens → IDs
forwardPass.tokenize.explanation: The tokenizer splits text into subword pieces using Byte Pair Encoding (BPE). Common words stay whole; rare words get split. "Classify" becomes "Class" + "ify" because the model learned these subwords are more efficient than storing every possible word.
forwardPass.embed.title: Embedding
forwardPass.embed.subtitle: Token IDs → 960-dimensional Vectors
forwardPass.embed.explanation: Each token ID is used to look up a 960-dimensional vector from the embedding table. This vector is the token's "meaning" in the model's learned representation space. Similar tokens end up with similar vectors.
forwardPass.position.title: Positional Encoding
forwardPass.position.subtitle: Adding Position Information
forwardPass.position.explanation: Transformers process all tokens in parallel, so they have no built-in sense of order. Positional encodings are added to tell the model where each token sits in the sequence. Without this, "IOPS: 45000" and "45000: IOPS" would look identical.
forwardPass.attention.title: Self-Attention
forwardPass.attention.subtitle: Connecting Tokens to Each Other
forwardPass.attention.explanation: Each token is projected into Query (what am I looking for?), Key (what do I contain?), and Value (what information should I share?) vectors. The dot product of Q and K produces attention scores — how much each token should "pay attention to" every other token. LoRA adapters insert into Q and V projections.
forwardPass.ffn.title: Feed-Forward Network
forwardPass.ffn.subtitle: Transform Each Token Independently
forwardPass.ffn.explanation: After attention mixes information between tokens, the feed-forward network processes each token independently. It first expands the representation to 4x width (3840 dims), applies a nonlinearity (GELU), then compresses back to 960. This is where the model "thinks" about each token's updated meaning.
forwardPass.predict.title: Next Token Prediction
forwardPass.predict.subtitle: Hidden State → Vocabulary Probabilities
forwardPass.predict.explanation: The final hidden state is projected to a score for every token in the 49,152-word vocabulary. Softmax converts these scores to probabilities. The token with the highest probability is selected as the output. Fine-tuning changes which token gets the highest probability — from generic words to the correct classification label.
forwardPass.steps.intro: Walk through how a Transformer processes a storage I/O prompt, one operation at a time. Each step builds on the previous one.
forwardPass.steps.splitNotice: Notice how "Classify" was split into "Class" + "ify" — the tokenizer breaks uncommon words into known subword pieces.
forwardPass.steps.embeddingIntro: Each token becomes a 960-dimensional vector. Below shows 8 representative dimensions — notice how "IOPS" and " 45" have similar patterns (they co-occur in training data):
forwardPass.steps.positionIntro: Without positional encoding, the model can't distinguish "IOPS: 45000" from "45000: IOPS". Position information is added to each embedding:
forwardPass.steps.positionWhy: <1>Why this matters:</1> Position 0 ("Class") and position 17 (the final ":") need different treatments. The model uses position to know that the end of the prompt is where it should generate the classification.
forwardPass.steps.attentionIntro: Each token is projected into three vectors. The Q·K dot product determines attention weights:
forwardPass.steps.loraInsertsHere: <1>LoRA inserts here:</1> Low-rank adapters are added alongside the Q and V projection matrices. Instead of modifying all 360M parameters, LoRA adds ~432K trainable parameters that adjust how queries and values are computed — changing what the model "looks for" and what information it "shares."
forwardPass.steps.ffnIntro: After attention mixes information between tokens, the FFN processes each token independently — expanding then compressing the representation:
forwardPass.steps.ffnWhy: <1>Why expand then compress?</1> The expansion to 3,840 dimensions gives the model a larger space to compute nonlinear transformations. The compression back to 960 forces it to distill the most important information. This is where the model "thinks" about each token's updated meaning after receiving context from attention.
forwardPass.steps.predictIntro: The final hidden state is projected to a probability over all 49,152 vocabulary tokens. Compare the base model (generic word prediction) vs. fine-tuned (task-specific classification):
forwardPass.steps.predictEffect: <1>The effect of fine-tuning:</1> The base model wants to generate generic continuation words ("The", "This", newlines). After SFT + DPO + GRPO, the model has learned that "OLTP" is the right answer for this I/O pattern — probability jumped from 3% to 73%. This is what all that post-training accomplished.

infraCard.heading: Infrastructure Profile
infraCard.scalingNote: Numbers shown for SmolLM2-360M. For 7B models, multiply GPU memory ~20x and training time ~15x.
train.heroP: Run the same training pipeline used to build this demo. You'll fine-tune a SmolLM2-360M language model to classify storage I/O workloads using SFT, DPO, and GRPO — then test it live in your browser.
train.googleColabP: Free cloud Jupyter environment with GPU access. Sign in with any Google account. The notebooks will prompt for login automatically.
train.huggingFaceP: Free account for hosting your trained model. Needed for the ONNX export step — the notebook uploads your model to your HuggingFace space.
train.clickToLearnMore: Click any stage to learn more.
train.step1Detail: Training data is generated synthetically from workload profiles — statistical distributions of I/O metrics (IOPS, throughput, latency, block size, read/write ratio, queue depth, sequential %) for each of 6 storage workload types. Each sample is a randomized draw from these distributions, formatted as a natural-language classification prompt. No external dataset is needed — the notebook generates fresh training data every run.
train.step2Detail: Supervised Fine-Tuning teaches the model WHAT to output. We show it ~1,400 prompt/completion pairs where the prompt describes I/O metrics and the completion is the correct workload label. SFT uses LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) — instead of updating all 360M parameters, we train small adapter matrices (~0.5% of total weights) that modify the model's behavior. Training takes ~12 minutes on a T4 GPU.
train.step3Detail: Direct Preference Optimization teaches the model HOW to respond. Given two possible outputs for the same prompt — one preferred ("chosen") and one not ("rejected") — DPO adjusts the model to favor the preferred style. This refines confidence and formatting without needing a separate reward model. It's the technique that made RLHF practical for most teams. Training takes ~8 minutes on T4.
train.step4Detail: Group Relative Policy Optimization is the technique behind DeepSeek R1. For each prompt, the model generates K=8 candidate responses. A reward function scores each one (1.0 for correct, 0.0 for wrong). The model learns from the group statistics — no human feedback needed. The answer itself is the teacher. This is where accuracy improves most dramatically. Training takes ~35 minutes on T4.
train.step5Detail: The trained LoRA adapter is merged back into the base model weights, then converted to ONNX format for browser inference. ONNX (Open Neural Network Exchange) is a portable format that runs on CPU/GPU/WebGPU without Python or PyTorch. The exported ~180MB model is uploaded to your HuggingFace space, where the browser app can download and run it client-side using transformers.js.
train.notebook1Desc: The full SFT → DPO → GRPO pipeline. Train a SmolLM2-360M model to classify storage I/O workloads, then export to ONNX for browser inference.
train.notebook1Time: ~60 min on T4, ~20 min on A100
train.notebook2Desc: Random Forest & XGBoost on the same classification task. Shows when traditional ML is the better choice — faster, smaller, and often more accurate on structured numeric data.
train.notebook3Desc: Unstructured storage error log classification — a task where LLMs genuinely outperform traditional ML. Compares TF-IDF + XGBoost against SFT fine-tuning.
train.sftNote: Label-only completions. Prompt tokens are masked in the loss.
train.dpoNote: Preference pairs teach style, not new knowledge.
train.grpoNote: K=8 candidates per prompt, scored by binary reward.
train.runtimeT4Detail: SFT ~12 min, DPO ~8 min, GRPO ~35 min, Export ~5 min
train.runtimeA100Detail: SFT ~4 min, DPO ~3 min, GRPO ~10 min, Export ~3 min
train.gpuMemoryDetail: LoRA adapters keep memory footprint small
train.outputSizeDetail: Two models exported: base (untrained) and GRPO (trained)
train.tryYourModelP: After training and exporting to ONNX, download the model to your browser and run inference locally. Compare the untrained base model against your GRPO-trained model.
results.heroP: Building a post-training pipeline from scratch — the honest results, the debugging journey, and when to use (and not use) an LLM.
results.rightTool: The Right Tool for the Right Job
results.structuredAdvice: If your features are numbers in a table, use a tree-based model.
results.unstructuredAdvice: If your input is natural language with variable phrasing, LLMs understand context that bag-of-words models miss.
results.accuracyCommentary: These numbers look low — and they are. That's the point. A 360M-parameter model classifying structured numeric data is the wrong tool for this job. Random Forest gets 97% in under a second. But the <1>techniques</1> we used (SFT, DPO, GRPO) are the same ones used to build ChatGPT and DeepSeek R1. The value is in learning the process, not in the accuracy on this particular task.
results.moreData: <1>More training data:</1> We used ~720 samples. Production fine-tuning uses 10K-100K+ examples.
results.largerModel: <1>Larger model:</1> SmolLM2-360M was chosen for browser inference. A 1.7B or 7B model would score significantly higher.
results.unstructuredInput: <1>Unstructured input:</1> On error log classification (text, not numbers), the same SFT technique achieves ~80-90%. The task matters more than the technique.
results.moreGrpoSteps: <1>More GRPO steps:</1> We ran 180 steps. DeepSeek R1 ran thousands. RL improves with more compute.
results.debug1Title: The Model Invented Its Own Labels
results.debug1Summary: Small models can't memorize valid outputs from training data alone
results.debug1Detail: During GRPO training, the 360M model generated classifications like "Vogeek Podcast", "Games Volumes", "RAID OPS", and "Fiction Writing" — labels that don't exist. The model had learned the concept of classification but not the vocabulary. Fix: include the 6 valid labels directly in the prompt. Key insight: small models need constraints in-context. This is a great example of why prompt engineering matters even after fine-tuning.
results.debug2Summary: The reward function was too rigid for freeform model output
results.debug2Detail: GRPO's accuracy curve was flat at 0% for all 180 training steps. The reward function only accepted exact matches like "Classification: OLTP Database", but the model generated freeform text with extra words, reasoning, and varied formatting. The model was actually learning — it was getting answers right — but the scoring function couldn't see it. Fix: robust extraction with keyword matching and fallback scanning. Lesson: in RL, the reward function IS the specification. If it's too narrow, the model can't learn.
results.debug3Title: The Invisible Space That Broke Everything
results.debug3Summary: A single trailing space caused a BPE tokenization mismatch
results.debug3Detail: SFT accuracy was stuck at ~20% despite the loss curve looking normal. The bug: our training prompts ended with "Classification: " (trailing space) but the completion started with " OLTP Database" (leading space). The tokenizer merged them into a single token, but at inference time the prompt's trailing space tokenized differently. The completion token the model had learned to generate didn't match what it saw. Fix: prompt ends with "Classification:" (no space), completion starts with " OLTP Database" (space). One character, 15% accuracy swing.
results.debug4Title: Temperature: The Knob Nobody Tells You About
results.debug4Summary: High sampling temperature caused hallucinations even with correct prompts
results.debug4Detail: Even after adding valid labels to the prompt, GRPO sampling at temperature=0.8 still produced garbage outputs. The model "knew" the right answer but the high randomness in sampling caused it to wander into nonsense tokens. Lowering to temperature=0.5 with top_p=0.85 eliminated extraction failures entirely. For RL-based training methods, sampling temperature is a critical hyperparameter — too high and the model can't learn from its own outputs, too low and it can't explore.
results.debug5Summary: The full pipeline required multiple re-runs and alignment across all stages
results.debug5Detail: We ran the pipeline at least 5 times end-to-end before getting reasonable results. Each fix (prompt format, temperature, extraction logic, BPE alignment) required re-training the ENTIRE pipeline — SFT, then DPO, then GRPO — because each stage builds on the previous one. Running GRPO with a new prompt format but an OLD SFT adapter produced worse results than starting over. This is the reality of post-training work: it's iterative, it requires alignment across every stage, and the debugging skills matter as much as the ML knowledge.
results.infraNote: GRPO takes 3x longer than SFT — reinforcement learning generates K=8 candidates per prompt and scores each one. This is where GPU burst capacity matters. For infrastructure teams: expect sustained GPU utilization during GRPO, not the bursty pattern of SFT.
results.takeaway1P: Structured numeric data → Random Forest. Unstructured text → LLM fine-tuning. Neither is universally better.
results.takeaway2P: 360M parameters can learn tasks but can't memorize valid outputs. Constraints must be in-context.
results.takeaway3P: Expect 3-5 full pipeline runs. Every change to prompts, hyperparameters, or evaluation requires retraining all downstream stages.
results.takeaway4Title: The reward function IS the specification
results.takeaway4P: In GRPO/RLVR, if your reward function can't score correctly, the model can't learn. Invest in robust evaluation.
results.takeaway5P: The same techniques that built ChatGPT run on a free Colab GPU in an hour. The barrier is knowledge, not hardware.
